content: A ligand which has two different donor atoms and either of the two ligates with the central metal atom/ion in the complex is called ___________
explanation:
The correct answer is Option D: Ambidentate ligand.

• An ambidentate ligand has two (or more) different donor atoms but binds through only one donor site at a time.

• Classic examples include SCN⁻ (thiocyanate, which can bind through S or N) and NO₂⁻ (nitrite, which can bind through N or O).

content:
Which of the following statements are true about $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ ?

(a) The complex has tetrahedral geometry

(b) Co-ordination number of Ni is 2 and oxidation state is +4

(c) The complex is $\mathrm{sp}^3$ hybridised

(d) It is a high spin complex

(e) The complex is paramagnetic
explanation:
$$ \left[\mathrm{Ni} \mathrm{Cl}_4\right]^{-2} $$

$\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{S}$ of $\mathrm{Ni}-+2$

Coordination no. of $\mathrm{Ni}^{+2}-4$

$\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$is weak field ligand, pairing does not take place

$\mathrm{Ni}^{+2}-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^8\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,4 s^{\circ}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,4 p^{\circ}$

$$ \left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{-2} \text { is paramagnetic } $$

content: $$ \text { Which formula and its name combination is incorrect? } $$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{CO}_3\right)\right] \mathrm{Cl}\\ &\text { Penta ammine carbonate cobalt (III) chloride. } \end{aligned} $$

content: $$ \text { In the complex ion }\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-} \text {, the co-ordination number of } \mathrm{Fe} \text { is } $$
explanation:
The oxalate ion (C₂O₄²⁻) is a bidentate ligand, meaning it donates two oxygen atoms to the central metal. In [Fe(C₂O₄)₃]³⁻:

Number of oxalate ligands = 3

Donor atoms per oxalate = 2

Thus, the coordination number of Fe is

$$3 \times 2 = 6.$$

Answer: 6 (Option C)

content: On treating 100 mL of 0.1 M aqueous solution of the complex $\mathrm{CrCl}_3 \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3, 2.86 \mathrm{~g}$ of AgCl was obtained. The complex is
explanation:
Given, Molarity of the complex $=0.1 \mathrm{M}$

Volume of the complex $=100 \mathrm{~mL}$

Mass of AgCl obtained $=2.86 \mathrm{~g}$

Now, no. of moles in $\mathrm{CrCl}_3 \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

$$=\frac{\text { molarity } \times \text { volume }}{1000}=\frac{0.1 \times 100}{1000}=0.01 \mathrm{moles}$$

No. of moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}=\frac{2.86}{143}=0.02$ moles

Number of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions present in the ionisation sphere

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\text { Moles of ions precipitated with excess } \mathrm{AgNO}_3}{\text { Moles of complex }} \\ & =\frac{0.02}{0.01}=2 \end{aligned}$$

It means two chlorine ions must be present in the solution. So, the complex will be

$$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2 \cdot \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$

content: The complex compounds $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{SO}_4\right] \mathrm{Br}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_4$ are
explanation:
The complex $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{SO}_4\right] \mathrm{Br}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_4$ are ionisation isomers of each other.

$\bullet$ This form of isomerism arises when the counter ion in a complex salt is itself a potential ligands and can displace a ligand which can then become the counter ion.

content:
Which of the following statements are true about $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$ ion ?

I. The complex has octahedral geometry.

II. Coordination number of Co is 3 and oxidation state is +6 .

III. The complex is $s p^3 d^2$ hybridised.

IV. It is a high spin complex.
explanation: Statements given in I, III and IV are true regarding $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$ ion while II is false. The correct form of statement II is coordination number of Co is 6 and its oxidation state is +3 .

content:
If a didentate ligand ethane-1, 2-diamine is progressively added in the molar ratio en : Ni :: 1: 1, 2 : 1, 3 : 1 to $$\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$$ aq solution, following co-ordination entities are formed.

I. $$\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_4 \mathrm{en}\right]^{2+}(a q)$$ - pale blue

II. $$\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_2(\mathrm{en})_2\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})$$ - blue/purple

III. $$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{en})_3\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})$$ - violet

The wavelength in $$\mathrm{nm}$$ of light absorbed in case of I and III are respectively
explanation: Given that, compound I shows pale blue colour. It means that, absorbed colour is orange whereas compound III shows violet colour then the absorbed colour is yellow. The wavelength of light absorbed by I and III are nearly $$600 \mathrm{~nm}$$ and $$535 \mathrm{~nm}$$ respectively.

content: Which of the following is an organometallic compound
explanation:
$$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{MgBr}$$ is considered as an organometallic compound because it contains a direct bond between a carbon atom and a metal atom (magnesium). Organometallic compounds contains at least one carbon-metal bond.

They can participate in variety of reactions and often used as reagents or catalysts in organic synthesis.

content:
When $$\mathrm{FeCl}_3$$ is added to excess of hot water gives a sol '$$X$$'. When $$\mathrm{FeCl}_3$$ is added to $$\mathrm{NaOH}(a q)$$ solution, gives sol '$$Y$$'

$$X$$ and $$Y$$ formed in the above processes respectively are
explanation:
When $$\mathrm{FeCl}_3$$ is added to an excess of hot water, it hydrolyses to form a sol '$$X$$' containing $$\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \cdot x \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$ and $$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$$ ions.

$$\mathrm{FeCl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \cdot x \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} / \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$$

When $$\mathrm{FeCl}_3$$ is added to $$\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{aq})$$ solution, a precipitation reaction occurs. The $$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$$ ions from $$\mathrm{FeCl}_3$$ react with $$\mathrm{OH}^{-}$$ ions from $$\mathrm{NaOH}$$ to form brown precipitate of $$\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_3$$. This brown precipitate forms a sol '$$Y$$' containing $$\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \cdot x \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$ and $$\mathrm{OH}^{-}$$ ions.

$$\mathrm{FeCl}_3+3 \mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \cdot x \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}+3 \mathrm{NaCl}$$

content: Which formula and name combination is incorrect?
explanation:
Formula and name combination given in option (c) is incorrect. Its correct form is as follows.

$$\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_2(\mathrm{en})_2\right] \mathrm{Cl}$$ - Dichloridebis(ethylene diamine) - cobalt (III) chloride.

content: Which of the following system in an octahedral complex has maximum unpaired electrons?
explanation: $$\therefore d^7$$ (high spin) system has maximum unpaired electrons.

content: The correct IUPAC name of cis-platin is
explanation:
The correct IUPAC name for cis-platin, a chemotherapy drug used to treat various types of cancers, is Option D: diamminedichloridoplatinum (II).

The reason for this naming is based on IUPAC rules for naming coordination complexes, which are as follows:

  • First, the ligands are named in alphabetical order, regardless of charge or the number of each type of ligand present.
  • The prefix "diammine" indicates the presence of two ammine (NH3) ligands.
  • The prefix "dichlorido" indicates the presence of two chlorido (Cl-) ligands.
  • Following the ligands, the metal atom is named, which in this case is platinum. The oxidation state of the metal is indicated by Roman numerals in parentheses immediately following the metal's name.

In cis-platin, the platinum has an oxidation state of +2. We know this because there are two ammonia molecules and two chloride ions as ligands, each contributing either to the neutrality or the charge balance of the complex:

[Pt(NH3)2Cl2]}

Solving for platinum's oxidation state:

Let $ x $ be the oxidation state of platinum. The ammonia ligands do not have a charge, but each chloride ion has a charge of -1. Therefore, we have:

x + 2(charge of NH3) + 2(-1) = 0

$$ x + 0 - 2 = 0 $$

$$ x = 2 $$

Thus, Option D, diamminedichloridoplatinum (II), is the correct IUPAC name, where (II) reflects the +2 oxidation state of the platinum in the complex.

content: Crystal field splitting energy (CFSE) for $$\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_6\right]^{4-}$$ is $$18000 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$$. The crystal field splitting energy (CFSE) for $$\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_4\right]^{2-}$$ will be
explanation:
$$\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_6\right]^{4-}$$ is an octahedral complex, its CFSE value, $$\Delta_0=18000 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$$

$$\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_4\right]^{2-}$$ is a tetrahedral complex, $$\Delta_t=$$ ?

We know that,

$$\Delta_t=\frac{4}{9} \Delta_0=\frac{4}{9} \times 18000=8000 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$$

content: The complex hexamineplatinum(IV)chloride will give _____ number of ions on ionisation.
explanation:
The formula of hexamineplatinum (IV) chloride is $$\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_4$$.

$$\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_4 \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{4+}+4 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$$

Thus, five ions are produced.

content: For the crystal field splitting in octahedral complexes,

options.content: the energy of the $$e_g$$ orbitals will decrease by $$(3 / 5) \Delta_o$$ and that of the $$t_{2 g}$$ will increase by $$(2 / 5) \Delta_0$$
the energy of the $$e_g$$ orbitals will increase by $$(3 / 5) \Delta_o$$ and that of the $$t_{2 g}$$ will decrease by $$(2/5) \Delta_o$$
the energy of the $$e_g$$ orbitals will increase by $$(3 / 5) \Delta_o$$ and that of the $$t_{2 g}$$ will increase by $$(2 / 5) \Delta_0$$
the energy of the $$e_g$$ orbitals will decrease by $$(3 / 5) \Delta_0$$ and that of the $$t_{2 g}$$ will decrease by $$(2 / 5) \Delta_0$$

explanation:
For crystal field splitting in octahedral complexes, the energy of $$e_g$$ orbitals will increase by $$\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) \Delta_0$$ and that of $$t_{2 g}$$ will decrease by $$\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \Delta_0$$.

The splitting of degenerate $$d$$-orbital is as follows.

content: The IUPAC name of $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{CO}_3\right)\right] \mathrm{Cl}$$ is
explanation:
$$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{CO}_3\right)\right] \mathrm{Cl}$$

Oxidation state of $$\operatorname{Co}(x)$$ can be calculated

$$\begin{aligned} & x+0+0-3=0 \\ & \therefore \quad x=3 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ The IUPAC name is pentaamminecarbonatocabolt(III)chloride.

content:
Homoleptic complexes among the following are

(A) $$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]$$

(B) $$\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_2(\mathrm{en})_2\right]^{+}$$

(C) $$\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{OH})_4\right]$$
explanation:
In homoleptic complex, all the bounded ligands are identicals

$$\therefore \mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]$$ and $$\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{OH})_4\right]$$ are homoleptic complex as they contain identical ligand i.e. $$\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)$$ and $$(\mathrm{OH})$$.

content: The correct order for wavelengths of light absorbed in the complex ions $$\left[\mathrm{CoCl}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$$ and $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$$ is
explanation:
Wavelengths of light absorbed is inversely proportional to strength of the ligand and order of strength of the given ligand is $$\mathrm{CN}^{-}>\mathrm{NH}_3>\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$$.

Therefore, the correct order for wavelength of light absorbed in the complex ions is

$$\left[\mathrm{CoCl}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{2+}>\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}>\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$$

content: The coordination number of $$\mathrm{Fe}$$ and $$\mathrm{Co}$$ in the complex ions, $$\left.\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$$ and $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{SCN})_4\right]^{2-}$$ are respectively
explanation:
The coordination number of Fe in $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3+}$$ is 6 and $$\mathrm{Co}$$ in $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{SCN})_4\right]^{2-}$$ is 4. It is because $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$$ is a bidentate ligand. So, $$3 \times 2=6$$

$$\mathrm{SCN}$$ is a monodendate ligand $$4 \times 1=4$$

So, depending upon the number of the ligands, we can calculate the coordination number of central metal atom.

content: Number of stereoisomers exhibited by $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}$$ is
explanation: The number of stereoisomers exhibited by $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]$$ is three.

content: Give the IUPAC name of $$\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]\left[\mathrm{Pt} \mathrm{Cl}_4\right]$$ is
explanation: The IUPAC name of $$\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_4\right]$$ is tetraammineplatinum (II) tetrachloridoplatinate (II).

content: Which among the following is the strongest ligand?
explanation: $$\mathrm{CO}$$ is the strongest ligand. It has empty $$\pi$$-orbitals with the correct symmetry to overlap with the metal $$t_{2 g}$$-orbitals, forming $$\pi$$ bonds. This is described as back bonding. Normally the $$\pi$$ orbitals on the ligands are of higher energy than the metal $$t_{2 g}$$ orbitals. No more electrons are added to the scheme as the ligand $$\pi$$-orbitals are empty, but the $\pi$ interaction increases the value of $$\Delta_0$$.

content: The formula of pentaaquanitratochromium (III) nitrate is,
explanation: The formula of pentaaquanitratochromium (III) nitrate is $$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_5\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)\right]\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2$$, while naming, the polydentate ligands are listed alpha betically. So, aqua is written first followed by nitrate.

content: The IUPAC name of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)\right] \mathrm{Cl}$ is
explanation:
The correct IUPAC name of the given coordinate compound is:

Tetraammine chloridonitrito-N-cobalt(III) chloride.

Hence, (a) is the correct option.

content: Square planar complex of the type $M_{\text {AXBL }}$ (where $A, B, X$ and $L$ ) are unidenate ligands shows following set of isomers
explanation: Square planar complex of the type $M_{A \times B \times L}$ shows two cis and one trans isomers.

content: The coordination number and the oxidation state of the. element ' $M$ ' in the complex $\left[\mathrm{M}(e n)_2\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)\right] \mathrm{NO}_2$ {where (en) is ethan1, 2-diamine) are respectively
explanation:
In the given coordination compound $\left[\text{M}(\text{en})_2(\text{C}_2\text{O}_4)\right] \text{NO}_2$, analyze the following:

Coordination Number:

The ligands present are:

en (ethane-1,2-diamine): This is a bidentate ligand, which means it can attach at two points to the metal center. Here, there are two en ligands, contributing a total of $2 \times 2 = 4$ coordination sites.

$\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$ (oxalate ion): This is also a bidentate ligand, contributing 2 coordination sites.

Adding these, the total coordination number is $4 + 2 = 6$.

Oxidation State of $M$:

Consider the charges:

The en ligand is neutral, contributing nothing to the net charge.

The oxalate ion $(\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-})$ contributes $-2$.

The nitrite ion outside the coordination sphere contributes $-1$.

Overall charge of the complex is balanced by the nitrite ion $\text{NO}_2^-$.

Let the oxidation state of $M$ be $x$:

Write the equation: $x + 0 + (-2) = -1$.

Simplify to find $x = +3$.

Therefore, the coordination number is 6, and the oxidation state of $M$ is +3.

content: According to crystal field theory, the $M-L$ bond in a complex is
explanation: According to crystal field theory considers the bond between metal ion and ligand as "purely electrostatic" i.e. this theory assumes that metal ion and ligands act as point charges and hence interaction between them is purely electrostatic or purely ionic in nature.
